parentQuestion: Rods absorb light throughout the visual spectrum but confer only gray tone vision.

scaffolds.q: Rods are the photoreceptors for dim light (scotopic) vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Rods are incredibly sensitive - can detect single photons in darkness.

scaffolds.q: Rods contain only ONE type of photopigment: Rhodopsin.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Unlike cones (3 types), rods have just one pigment.

scaffolds.q: Rhodopsin absorbs photons across the visible spectrum.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Peak at ~498nm (blue-green), but responds to all visible wavelengths.

scaffolds.q: A rod can't tell WHAT wavelength it absorbed - just that it absorbed a photon.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! One photopigment = no wavelength discrimination. Just counts photons.

scaffolds.q: Color perception requires COMPARING different photoreceptor types.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! You need multiple channels with different spectral sensitivities to decode color.

scaffolds.q: With only rods active (dim light), you see in shades of gray - no color.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! 'All cats are gray in the dark' - rod vision is achromatic.

scaffolds.q: Rods report intensity, not wavelength. Gray scale, not color.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! One channel can only report 'how much' light, not 'what kind' of light.

scaffolds.q: Rods absorb all visible wavelengths but can't distinguish them - only gray tones.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Single pigment type = no wavelength comparison = achromatic vision.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - rods absorb all wavelengths but only produce gray tone vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is the 'univariance principle.' One photoreceptor type conflates wavelength and intensity - can't tell them apart. Color requires multiple channels for comparison.
